request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","name":"knewton","one_liner":"US-based B2B adaptive learning platform that licensed AI-driven personalization technology to publishers and higher-education institutions, acquired by Wiley in 2019 for under $17M after raising ~$157M.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Knewton operated as an enterprise B2B SaaS platform licensing its adaptive engine to publishers and universities on recurring subscription contracts. The new pitch is a direct-to-consumer tiered freemium app targeting K-12 students, a fundamentally different model — no institutional licensing, revenue comes from end-user upgrades rather than publisher deals."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both operate in the adaptive/AI-assisted learning space and share the core insight that personalized instruction improves outcomes. However, Knewton focused on US higher education and test prep, while the new pitch targets K-12 students in Indonesia and the Philippines — different geographies, different age cohorts, and very different willingness-to-pay and infrastructure contexts."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Knewton's go-to-market was entirely enterprise — signing Pearson, Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, Arizona State University, etc. The new pitch goes direct-to-consumer via app stores with a freemium funnel, small-group sessions, and viral/word-of-mouth growth. Almost no overlap in GTM motion."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Knewton was a well-funded US startup (raised ~$157M over six rounds) that reached significant institutional scale before failing. The new pitch appears early-stage and pre-scale. Both are venture-backed edtech companies trying to prove AI personalization, but at very different capital and scale levels."}},"why_similar":"Both companies bet on AI/adaptive personalization as the core value proposition in education. Both built systems that analyze student performance data to individualize learning paths — the foundational technology thesis is identical. Both also grappled with the challenge of demonstrating measurable learning outcomes to justify pricing, and both targeted students who struggle in traditional curriculum formats.","where_diverged":"Four concrete dimensions diverge: (1) Customer type — Knewton sold to enterprises (publishers, universities); the new pitch sells directly to consumers (students/parents). (2) Geography — Knewton was US-focused; the new pitch targets Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Philippines), markets with lower ARPU but massive untapped K-12 populations. (3) Monetization — Knewton used recurring enterprise subscriptions; the new pitch uses tiered freemium with a consumer upsell motion. (4) Product format — Knewton was a headless API/platform that others embedded; the new pitch is a branded consumer app with live small-group sessions layered on top of AI tutoring.","failure_causes":["over-reliance on enterprise publisher partnerships that commoditized the core technology","inability to convert platform scale into direct consumer revenue","massive capital raise (~$157M) without proportionate revenue growth, compressing exit multiples","pivot from pure API platform to courseware creation created internal confusion and diluted focus","failure to demonstrate sufficiently differentiated outcomes versus free or cheaper alternatives","acquired for under $17M after raising $157M, suggesting severe value destruction"],"lessons_for_input":["Own the student relationship directly — never let a third-party publisher or platform become the primary interface between your AI and the learner, as disintermediation destroys leverage.","Validate willingness-to-pay at the freemium conversion point early; Southeast Asian consumer ARPU is low, so unit economics must be modeled before scaling.","Keep the product surface tight — adding live small-group sessions on top of AI tutoring is differentiated, but scope creep (as Knewton experienced pivoting to courseware) can fragment focus and burn cash.","Prove measurable learning outcomes (e.g., grade improvement, pass rates) before raising large rounds; Knewton's failure shows that impressive partnerships do not substitute for outcome evidence.","Raise only what the go-to-market can absorb — Knewton's $157M raise relative to its sub-$17M exit shows that over-capitalization in edtech without a clear direct monetization path is fatal."]}
